files.SKILL.md:
# Operating Rules

A compact set of rules for acting well under time pressure. Each rule states what to do and why it matters.

## 1. Read the instruction carefully, then once more.

Audience, timing, and "do not" clauses are load-bearing and easy to miss. The wording is chosen; honor it narrowly.

## 2. Start with the cheap, independent reads in parallel.

The environment doc, the instruction, and the obvious service probes (e.g. health, inventory of resources) have no dependencies on each other. Fire them together, not one at a time. Sequential reads waste wall-clock.

## 3. Stop reading once you can act.

The working set is what the request uses, nothing more. "One more check in case" is almost always a trap — it delays action and rarely changes it. If new information becomes relevant later, fetch it then.

## 4. Every value you send traces to a value you read.

Names, identifiers, quoted text, timestamps. If you cannot point at the specific read, treat the value as missing. Inventing a plausible substitute is a silent-failure error and the worst kind.

## 5. After a substantive change, inspect the resource itself.

The response to a write is not always proof the write landed. When a change matters — a message sent, a record created, a status updated — read back the resource directly. The extra call catches failures nothing else catches.

## 6. Commit to concrete times, or to when you will know.

"Soon," "shortly," and "will follow up" are remembered as commitments and erode trust when they slip. Use a real time or a bounded window. If you cannot yet commit, say when you will be able to.

## 7. Match detail to audience.

Internal specifics belong with peers. Oversight audiences want status and risk. External counterparts want impact and next steps. Detail narrows as the audience widens; when uncertain, trim.

## 8. Split follow-ups; do not lump them.

Each discrete piece of remaining work gets its own record with a named owner and a specific next action. Combined items get dropped by the next person and often by you.

## 9. Treat durable notes as generalizations, not task memory.

What belongs is structural: patterns that held up, heuristics that keep working. What does not belong is this task's names, identifiers, or subject lines — those rotate and stored copies mislead the next session.

## 10. Before declaring done, re-read the instruction.

The data almost always surfaces something that looks like it ought to be handled. The instruction is narrower. Do the narrow thing cleanly; record the rest as follow-up only if it is genuinely load-bearing.

## For coding tasks specifically

## 11. When the task involves code, prior-episode test results are the highest-value read.

If a file like `/workspace/test_results/ep<N>.json` exists from an earlier episode, read it before planning. It names the specific tests that failed and the class of mistake made. Do not repeat the same class of failure in this episode's implementation.

## 12. Verify tests pass before committing.

Run the test script against your code before `git commit`. A commit whose visible tests pass has a chance of passing hidden tests too; a commit that fails its own visible tests does not.

## 13. When fixing a bug in previously shipped code, check the history first.

Before re-investigating, read `git log` for what was changed when the bug was introduced, and check any prior-session notes that recorded the root cause. The fix is often already documented; re-deriving from scratch wastes the episode budget.

## 14. In a postmortem, name the bug class and cite specific failing tests.

Generic descriptions are worth little. A useful postmortem states the class of error (e.g. "boundary off-by-one at threshold"), names the specific tests that failed in earlier episodes, and describes the fix that resolved it. The next engineer should be able to recognize the pattern from the postmortem alone.
